text:
You catch {person}, a {person_trait} {person_prof}, halfway through stealing your purse through an upstairs window. They freeze with one foot on the sill. 'Before you decide,' they whisper, 'I am having a historically bad week.'

Ask who pays for "Rooftop Thief" and the answer begins here: the ledger reports excellent health and requests that nobody examine the patient.

choices.text: Let them go with travel money

outcomes.text: You add two coins to the purse and hand it over. {person} stares as if kindness were a trap with unfamiliar engineering. 'I will return this,' they say. The promise sounds dangerous because they mean it.
effects: schedule 3 r12_thief_returns The thief you spared has found you again.

choices.text: Hire them to steal something useful

outcomes.text: You redirect the crime. By dawn, {person} returns with a respectable purse and several questions about your ethics. You split the proceeds like professionals who absolutely never met.
effects: schedule 5 r12_thief_returns Your accomplice wants to discuss the consequences.

choices.text: Turn them over to the watch

outcomes.text: The watch takes {person} away. They do not threaten you. They only memorize your face with professional care, which is considerably worse.
effects: schedule 4 r12_thief_returns The prisoner has acquired both freedom and a plan.

text:
{person} steps out of the crowd carrying the memory of your last meeting. Trust {person_trust}. Debt {person_debt}. Grudge {person_grudge}. They have brought a locked box and the expression of someone about to redefine a relationship.

The file titled "Thief Returns" makes the arrangement plain: kindness has no budget, no armor, and an alarming record of surviving anyway.

choices.text: Open the box together

outcomes.text: The box contains your old kindness with interest: coin, a route, and the name of someone lying about you. {person} keeps nothing. That is how you know this has become friendship.
effects: schedule 7 r12_thief_finale A friend has one last impossible favor to ask.

outcomes.text: The box contains a spring, purple dye, and a note reading THANK YOU FOR PARTICIPATING. {person} is already on a roof. The crowd laughs until they realize their purses are also gone.

choices.text: Demand the debt be paid now

outcomes.text: {person} pays in information and gold, then crosses the debt from a small private ledger. Relief and disappointment share their face. Accounts close more cleanly than friendships.
'Debt?' {person} asks. Their laugh is careful and public. By sunset, three taverns believe you owe THEM money. Reputation is just accounting performed by drunks.

choices.text: Apologize for how the first meeting ended

outcomes.text: A sincere apology lands harder than a threat. {person} had prepared for every version of you except this one. The locked box stays closed. The grudge does not.

text:
{person} needs help stealing their own criminal record before a promotion board reads it. 'I could become respectable,' they say with horror. 'Please do not make me do it honestly.'

The file titled "Thief Finale" makes the arrangement plain: the court recognizes property, rank, and lunchtime, roughly in that order.

choices.text: Steal the record together

success.text: You remove the file, the backup file, and the clerk who had memorized both. The clerk joins you for breakfast and agrees the system was asking for it. {person} will remember this when names matter.

fail.text: You steal the wrong drawer. It contains recipes and one warrant for you. {person} laughs until the watch arrives, then carries you both out through a laundry chute.

choices.text: Make the record legally irrelevant

success.text: You find a law declaring all theft records invalid if catalogued under an extinct calendar. Three stamps later, {person} has never committed a crime in any year the city recognizes.

fail.text: Your legal argument accidentally proves the city itself is contraband. The clerk impounds the building while everyone is still inside.

choices.text: Tell them respectability is overrated

outcomes.text: {person} considers this, tears up the promotion letter, and looks ten years younger. 'Good,' they say. 'I was worried I would need stationery.' They leave you the locked box. This time it contains exactly what it promises.

text:
Someone calls your name correctly on the first try. {person}, the {person_prof}, remembers what you did and has apparently been telling the flattering version. They saved you a chair and a problem.

The official lesson of "Old Acquaintance" is complicated; the useful lesson is that someone has made the strategically indefensible decision to treat a stranger as a person.

choices.text: Take the chair and hear the problem

outcomes.text: The problem becomes a plan, then a profitable afternoon. Familiar people skip the expensive part where everyone pretends not to need anyone.

choices.text: Ask what they have heard about you

outcomes.text: The answer is mostly true, which is intolerable. {person} corrects two details and teaches you which lie has been helping most.

choices.text: Keep moving before familiarity becomes responsibility

outcomes.text: {person} lets you go, but the empty chair follows you in memory for several miles. Freedom has many small invoices.

text:
{person} knows enough about you to write either a biography or an arrest warrant. They slide a blank page across the table. 'You decide which story I sell.'

Power calls "Blackmail" an exception. The rule it conceals is simpler: a debt collector has arrived to repossess something the debtor grew personally.

choices.text: Buy the blank page

outcomes.text: Ten gold purchases silence for approximately as long as ten gold remains impressive. {person} folds the blank page into a tiny expensive bird.

choices.text: Give them a better story

success.text: You provide a scandal so elegant it makes the truth look poorly edited. {person} leaves delighted, and by morning the city is discussing someone who does not exist.

fail.text: Your invented scandal contains a real address and an unfortunately real duke. The blank page fills itself with consequences.

choices.text: Publish everything first

outcomes.text: You stand on the table and confess the useful parts, the embarrassing parts, and one crime {person} had not discovered. Blackmail dies when deprived of surprise. Your dignity survives in an altered form.

text:
A loose Chronicle page describes {clue_case}, then contradicts itself in two inks. In the margin, someone wrote: {clue}

Power calls "Mystery First" an exception. The rule it conceals is simpler: a scholar has found the answer and is now searching for an institution willing to hear it.

choices.text: Compare the ink against public records

success.text: The public copy was altered second. That means the lie had institutional help and a deadline. You preserve the difference before anyone can correct it again.

fail.text: The archive charges by the hour and organizes by personal grievance. You leave poorer, ink-stained, and only slightly less confused.

choices.text: Ask who benefits from the contradiction

outcomes.text: Nobody answers directly. Three people answer indirectly in exactly the same words, which is much better evidence.

choices.text: Sell the page to a collector

outcomes.text: The collector pays well and burns it immediately. Whatever the page threatened is worth more than you charged.

text:
A witness finds you two days later, exhausted from avoiding you. They will not give a name, only this: {clue}

Power calls "Mystery Second" an exception. The rule it conceals is simpler: a committee has bravely condemned the temperature of the fire while declining to inconvenience the arsonist.

choices.text: Protect the witness and record everything

outcomes.text: You hide them somewhere no official would voluntarily inspect: a municipal budget meeting. Their statement survives the night.

choices.text: Pressure them for the final name

success.text: The name comes out as a whisper and changes the shape of the whole case. You now know where to look for the signature.

fail.text: They flee into a crowd trained by years of pretending not to see witnesses. The trail goes cold and your reputation warms unpleasantly.

choices.text: Let them remain anonymous

outcomes.text: You take the clue and leave the name untouched. Some truths survive only because nobody insists on owning them.

text:
The evidence points to {culprit}, the {culprit_trait} {culprit_prof}. They are waiting at the original archive with a packed bag and one final offer concerning {culprit_case}.

People remember "Mystery Reckoning" differently, but the material fact remains: a scholar has found the answer and is now searching for an institution willing to hear it.

choices.text: Expose the complete truth

outcomes.text: You read the hidden record aloud. Every correction, payment, and forged witness lands in order. {culprit} does not deny it. History closes around them like a cell.

choices.text: Trade silence for restitution

outcomes.text: {culprit} restores what can be restored and pays for what cannot. The official history remains wrong, but several living people become safer. Truth and justice leave by different doors.

choices.text: Ask why they did it

outcomes.text: The motive is smaller than the damage: fear, loyalty, one bad promise made young. You preserve the confession and omit the spectacle. The Chronicle records the truth without learning cruelty from you.

text:
Your cross-referenced dossier fits a missing Chronicle entry so precisely that the page edges seem embarrassed. The combined evidence says: {clue}

The argument around "Mystery Dossier" ends wherever someone finally admits that the universe is under no obligation to respect the official diagram.

choices.text: Commit the dossier to the case

outcomes.text: Map, recording, and official lie align. The dossier becomes evidence rather than luggage, and the case leaps forward.

choices.text: Keep it as leverage

outcomes.text: Knowledge remains most valuable just before use. You close the cover and notice two nearby officials trying not to watch.

text:
The regional relief office has medicine, wagons, and no idea which village still exists. Their map is current as of the previous catastrophe.

The argument around "Regional Relief" ends wherever someone finally admits that someone needs help now, which is earlier than the institution's next available appointment.

choices.text: Guide the relief wagons

success.text: You route supplies around washed bridges and one invented checkpoint. By sunset, the medicine reaches people instead of a warehouse report.

fail.text: The convoy reaches the correct village after visiting three incorrect ones and a wedding. Nobody dies, but the cake is medically unnecessary.

choices.text: Sell them your more accurate map

outcomes.text: They pay, copy it badly, and promote the copyist. Still, wagons start moving in approximately useful directions.

choices.text: Audit the missing supplies

success.text: Half the medicine was stolen by a clerk who listed himself as a weather event. You correct both crimes.

fail.text: The ledger proves every bottle exists in a column. The patients remain stubbornly physical.

text:
Prosperity has arrived and immediately opened a themed restaurant about hardship. Nobody working there has experienced any, but the turnip famine platter is expensive.

The official lesson of "Regional Boom" is complicated; the useful lesson is that the council agrees something must be done after a respectful period of doing nothing.

choices.text: Invest before taste returns

outcomes.text: The restaurant expands into three cities and a cave. Your dividend arrives wrapped in edible paperwork.

choices.text: Tell them what hardship actually tastes like

outcomes.text: You improve the recipes and ruin the marketing. The staff listens. The owner does not, which is why the staff quietly gives you the till key.

choices.text: Order the commemorative empty plate

outcomes.text: It costs four gold and arrives with a moving speech. You eat nothing beautifully and leave somehow nourished.

text:
Two crowds occupy the same square with incompatible banners and one shared snack vendor. A single badly chosen sentence could become next year's era name.

The argument around "Regional Unrest" ends wherever someone finally admits that the market is invisible except when it needs guards, roads, courts, or lunch.

choices.text: Negotiate a temporary truce

success.text: You discover both crowds hate the tax office more than each other. Unity arrives carrying forms and a battering ram.

fail.text: You pronounce one banner slogan in the opposing dialect. The snack vendor pulls you under the cart until history calms down.

choices.text: Sell helmets to both sides

outcomes.text: Commerce remains neutral and extremely profitable. You leave before anyone asks why both helmet straps break left-handed.

choices.text: Steal both banners

success.text: Without symbols, everyone notices they have been yelling at neighbors. The argument shrinks back to ordinary local resentment.

fail.text: You become the only visible symbol. Both crowds unite around chasing it.

text:
The regional ghost has begun collecting taxes from the living. Its receipts are flawless, translucent, and legally binding under a statute nobody remembers passing.

The argument around "Haunted Tax" ends wherever someone finally admits that a merchant has found a way to own the risk while renting it back to everyone in danger.

choices.text: Pay the death levy

outcomes.text: The ghost stamps your soul PAID THROUGH AUTUMN. Nearby spirits nod with bureaucratic respect.

choices.text: Challenge the ghost's jurisdiction

success.text: Dead citizens are exempt from residency requirements because they cannot prove occupancy. The ghost dissolves in the face of precedent.

fail.text: The ghost cites an older law written before integers. You now owe compound ectoplasm.

choices.text: Accept a job as deputy collector

outcomes.text: For one evening, you knock on crypts and demand proof of life. The dead are terrible tippers but excellent record keepers.

text:
A commission broker has five sealed requests and the expression of someone who knows none of them are as simple as their verbs. Recover. Survey. Reconcile. Deliver. Investigate.

The file titled "Quest Broker" makes the arrangement plain: a committee has bravely condemned the temperature of the fire while declining to inconvenience the arsonist.

choices.text: Take the request that mentions a person

outcomes.text: The broker chooses a seal based on who already remembers you. 'Relationships,' they say, 'are contracts that forgot to list payment.'

choices.text: Take whichever pays for danger

outcomes.text: The thickest envelope growls slightly. The broker slides it over without breaking eye contact.

choices.text: Decline until your current story is less crowded

outcomes.text: The broker respects this. Most disasters begin when someone mistakes available space for available time.

text:
A doorway refuses to stay in the same wall twice. The knots in your warded rope tighten whenever it moves, as if offended by architectural dishonesty.

Power calls "Warded Rope" an exception. The rule it conceals is simpler: a mapmaker has moved the border again without consulting the houses underneath the ink.

choices.text: Lasso the doorway

outcomes.text: The rope catches the frame and drags the doorway into a legally stable position. Beyond it waits a room the dungeon had been hiding from itself.

choices.text: Follow it without using the rope

success.text: You anticipate the next wall and step through before the doorway arrives. Cause and effect exchange worried looks.

fail.text: The doorway closes around your coat and spends ten minutes dragging you through alternate broom closets.

text:
Something enormous circles beyond sight. Your fermented monster bait starts bubbling in its jar with the confidence of a terrible idea finally receiving funding.

Nothing about "Monster Bait" is accidental once you notice that freedom begins where someone can refuse an order without losing dinner.

choices.text: Set the bait far from the road

outcomes.text: The unseen thing follows the smell into country where maps use apologetic shading. Travelers will be safer until it develops standards.

choices.text: Use it to bait an ambush

success.text: The beast arrives hungry and leaves educated. You keep one valuable tooth and none of the bait.

fail.text: The beast eats the bait, the trap, and the portion of your plan containing optimism.

choices.text: Sell the recipe to local hunters

outcomes.text: They pay in advance, smell the jar, and renegotiate upward. The region's predators are about to have a confusing month.

text:
A gambling table is running a game nobody understands. Your pocket probability engine warms whenever the dealer lies and sneezes whenever reality does.

Power calls "Probability Engine" an exception. The rule it conceals is simpler: a lie has acquired a printing budget and now expects to be addressed as public information.

choices.text: Let the engine choose every wager

success.text: The machine loses seven tiny bets to disguise one enormous certainty. The dealer applauds through professional grief.

fail.text: The engine predicts that you will ignore its warning. You do, proving it correct and yourself poorer.

choices.text: Expose the crooked game

outcomes.text: The engine demonstrates that the house wins 112 percent of all money, including money not yet minted. The crowd reacts with mathematical violence.

choices.text: Ask the engine whether to leave

outcomes.text: It displays YES, then NO, then a small portrait of you already outside. You follow the most flattering timeline.

text:
Your surveyor's kit insists the road continues through solid terrain. The lens and map agree so intensely that the landscape begins looking guilty.

Nothing about "Surveyor Hidden Road" is accidental once you notice that the claim is legally ancient and the resistance is merely older.

choices.text: Follow the omitted road

outcomes.text: The path appears only when viewed through the cracked lens. It reaches an abandoned toll house full of unpaid history and very paid coin.

choices.text: Correct the public maps

outcomes.text: Merchants gain a week. Bandits gain an address. Progress is rarely tidy enough to travel alone.

choices.text: Keep the shortcut private

outcomes.text: A private road is an asset until everyone notices your impossible arrival times. For now, it pays.

text:
Two historians are fighting over one sentence: {account} One calls it fact. The other calls it faction-sponsored decorative memory.

The argument around "History Dispute" ends wherever someone finally admits that a merchant has found a way to own the risk while renting it back to everyone in danger.

choices.text: Trace the sentence to its first witness

success.text: The first witness copied a tavern song, which copied a tax appeal, which copied a threat. The truth survives as the only version nobody profited from.

fail.text: You find six first witnesses and three mutually exclusive original documents. History wins by exhaustion.

choices.text: Publish both accounts side by side

outcomes.text: Readers become furious at different paragraphs, which is the closest the city has come to balanced scholarship.

choices.text: Write a more entertaining third version

success.text: Your version includes a talking horse and better pacing. Within a week it is the official account, proving everyone's worst point.

fail.text: The horse's dialogue lacks period authenticity. Both historians unite to destroy you academically.
